rationales.self_direction: She chose to keep pursuing a PhD "at fifty-eight" even though others expected her to "enjoy retirement properly," showing commitment to her own path over others' expectations.
rationales.achievement: Her focus on the overdue "chapter draft due next week," fear of "feeling like a failure," and drive to keep "chasing citations" show strong investment in excelling academically.
rationales.benevolence: She spent the day at the hospital with Mother, then made sure "she ate lunch and took her pills," clearly prioritizing a close family member's care.

rationales.self_direction: She feels she cannot freely choose for herself, saying "it falls to me. It always falls to me," and immediately abandons her own plans when asked.
rationales.hedonism: She gives up the weekend she had been anticipating "the way you look forward to a small holiday," replacing rest and pleasure with exhausting caretaking.
rationales.achievement: She sacrifices the writing weekend she had reserved to "finally sit down and write," and admits bringing the laptop will not realistically lead to work getting done.
rationales.benevolence: She said yes "before she finished the sentence" when her exhausted daughter asked for help, putting her daughter's immediate needs first.

rationales.hedonism: She lingers on "twenty minutes of sunshine and soft dough" and her daughter laughing, treating those pleasurable moments as what "felt like breathing."
rationales.achievement: She reports "Wrote nothing" after the trip, showing her academic goals were set aside rather than advanced.
rationales.benevolence: The entire weekend was spent helping her daughter and baby, even at the cost of sleep and work, showing care for close family.

rationales.hedonism: She is openly excited about the upcoming hot pot dinner and says she is "looking forward to it more than I probably should," signaling real anticipation of enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: She "Submitted the draft" despite stress and immediately gauges the advisor's feedback, showing active follow-through on an academic goal.
rationales.benevolence: She made congee for Mother and values her praise, reflecting attentive care for a close family member.

rationales.hedonism: She centers sensory enjoyment throughout: the "wonderful" hot pot, ordering "a second round of the taro dumplings," buying pineapple cakes, and feeling "no guilt about it even once."

rationales.self_direction: She refuses to let her brother dictate her priorities and hangs up instead of automatically complying with family demands.
rationales.achievement: She explicitly defends being "in the middle of my comprehensive exams," treating her studies as a serious commitment worth protecting.
rationales.conformity: "I hung up on him. First time I've ever done that" shows a break from her usual role of smoothing things over and maintaining family harmony.

rationales.self_direction: She longs for choices that are "clean" and imagines letting "someone else handle Mother's birthday," showing a push to reclaim autonomy from constant obligation.
rationales.achievement: She frames the degree as "something entirely for myself" that she has wanted "since I was thirty," indicating sustained commitment to a personally meaningful goal.
rationales.conformity: She is angry at the family role she "keep[s] accepting" and wants to remember this feeling the next time she is expected to say yes, signaling resistance to expected self-sacrifice.

rationales.self_direction: She sets a boundary by telling her brother she will do the restaurant booking but "the guest list and gifts are his responsibility this year," rather than absorbing everything herself.
rationales.hedonism: She deliberately savors a "proper cup of oolong" on the balcony under a blanket, and describes the moment as a "Small pleasure" that felt earned.

rationales.achievement: She studied "until closing" the night before comprehensive exams and is mentally preparing to perform, showing disciplined pursuit of academic success.

rationales.self_direction: She embraces the degree even though it "might be impractical at my age" and says she finally felt like the self she imagined at twenty-five, showing ownership of her own life path.
rationales.hedonism: She plans to enjoy the French restaurant, new dress, and "every course without thinking about calories or cost," explicitly giving herself permission to savor the moment.
rationales.achievement: She "Passed" her exams, was able to answer difficult questions, and values recognition that her perspective "the literature lacks," all of which reflect strong academic accomplishment.

rationales.power: He forcefully "shut it down" when an outside consultant was proposed, treating outside influence over strategy as an unacceptable challenge to his authority.
rationales.security: After the company "nearly got swallowed six months ago," he justifies being highly protective about who gets access to strategy, showing a strong focus on guarding stability against threat.
rationales.conformity: He admits he may have shut the suggestion down "maybe too hard" in front of everyone, disregarding smoother, norm-respecting conduct in the meeting.

rationales.power: He is preoccupied by trusted colleagues leaving for "the enemy camp," an event that directly signals loss of influence and command over his organization.
rationales.security: His sleepless rumination about loyalty and whether it was "all just leverage" shows his sense of stability and trust has been badly shaken.

rationales.power: He says the company is where "people listen" and where he matters, and fears becoming "irrelevant" if that changes, revealing a strong attachment to influence, status, and being needed.
Even in a personal betrayal, he is "calculating the whole time" who saw the interaction and what Pieter might gain, showing fixation on status, leverage, and positional control.
rationales.conformity: He "kept my composure" and performed the expected polite response despite his anger, carefully restraining himself to preserve social appearance.

rationales.hedonism: He "Skipped the dinner" and spent the evening unable to enjoy himself, framing professional performance as something that crowds out simple comfort and pleasure.

rationales.achievement: He highlights that the team delivered "8% growth despite the disruption" and that they "exceeded expectations," emphasizing successful performance against standards.
rationales.security: He ends by stressing "The company is steady. That's what matters," showing a clear preference for stability and dependable footing.

rationales.achievement: He coaches Sophie to improve the pitch because if "she lands this client, it's a win for us," tying his support directly to stronger performance and results.
rationales.benevolence: He notices her nerves, lets her finish "without interrupting," and gives advice that she receives like "a map," showing active support for someone within his circle.

rationales.power: He insists on reviewing reports because he "need[s] to know everything" and fears what happens if he does not, reflecting a strong need to retain control.
rationales.security: He worries that without constant oversight "a client could be unhappy" and a problem might grow before he catches it, showing threat-sensitive concern for stability.
rationales.benevolence: He admits he could make time for Lucas but chooses reports instead, prioritizing company oversight over being present for his grandson.

rationales.power: He recognizes he spent thirty years making sure he was "irreplaceable" and kept others away from "the core," which directly reflects a drive to preserve unique control.
rationales.security: His 4 AM panic about succession and admission that "there's no one" to run the company if he dies reveals a serious lack of stability and preparedness.

rationales.self_direction: He makes a deliberate change by calling Maarten and choosing a "middle path" instead of staying trapped in his old pattern of total control.
rationales.power: Asking Maarten to take on "more strategic responsibility" means giving up some exclusive authority rather than keeping all control centered on himself.

rationales.self_direction: He intentionally experiments with a different way of operating, telling Marijke he was "trying something new" instead of defaulting to his old habits.
rationales.hedonism: He goes home at 5 PM "for the first time in months," allowing himself leisure and life outside the office.
rationales.power: He lets Maarten run the meeting and "Didn't intervene," actively stepping back from direct control.

rationales.stimulation: He tries something unfamiliar enough that he had to watch a YouTube video "on how to bait a hook," embracing a new and slightly uncomfortable experience.
rationales.hedonism: He treasures the fishing trip as "fun" and says the time with Lucas "was worth more than any contract I've ever closed," clearly valuing present enjoyment.
rationales.power: He questions the boardroom identity of being the one "who knows all the answers" and "who everyone defers to," moving away from status and control as the main source of worth.
rationales.benevolence: He spends hours simply listening to Lucas and values that connection over business success, showing attentive care for a close family relationship.
